revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[fr:Amoghapasha Lokeshvara]][[ja:不空羂索観音]][[simple:Amoghapasha]][[zh:不空羂索觀音]]


[[File:Amoghapasa_Padang_Roco_Inscription_Front.JPG|thumb|404x404px| Statue of Amoghapasha found in Padang Roco, {{ill|10=fr|11=Kabupaten_de_Dharmasraya|Dharmasraya Regency|en|User:Immanuelle/draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=fr|qq=}}, {{ill|West Sumatra|en|West Sumatra|qid=Q2772|lt=West Sumatra|1=West Sumatra}} province of {{ill|Indonesia|en|Indonesia|qid=Q252|lt=Indonesia|1=Indonesia}}.]]{{complex|qq=}}
'''{{ill|Amoghapasha|ja|不空羂索観音|fr|Amoghapasha Lokeshvara|id|Arca Amoghapasa|jv|Arca Amoghapasa|zh|不空羂索觀音|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amoghapasha|qq=}}''' ( IAST : '''Amoghapāśa''' ) is a {{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|qid=Q178149|lt=Bodhisattva|1=Bodhisattva}} and one of the manifestations or forms of {{ill|10=fr|11=Avalokiteśvara|Avalokiteśvara|en|User:Immanuelle/draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=fr|qq=}} (also '''Lokeshvara''' ), who can take on a feminine or masculine aspect. His name in Sanskrit means “he who has the unshakable cord (amoghapasha)” ; this lace which he generally holds in one hand represents his power to fish out lost beings far from the path. Its Chinese name is ''Bukongjuansuo Guanyin''不空羂索觀音, Japanese ''Fukūkensaku Kannon'' <ref>aussi ''Fukūkenjaku Kannon''</ref> . This name literally means ''Amoghapāśa-avalokiteśvara'' .
The oldest known text mentioning him is ''Amoghapasha hridaya sutra'' translated into Chinese by [[:en:Jnanagupta|Jnanagupta]] in 587, which became the first chapter of a work describing the various rituals concerning him, ''Amoghapasha kalparaja'' . Its first known figures date from the VIII century. century . He is most often represented with four, six or eight arms, and sometimes four servants including {{ill|Tara (Buddhism)|en|Tara (Buddhism)|qid=Q746709|lt=Tara|1=Tara (Buddhism)}} and {{ill|10=fr|11=Hayagriva|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|lt=Hayagriva|en|User:Immanuelle/draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=fr|qq=}} <ref> J A Schoterman ''Ancient Indonesian Sculpture'', Marijke J. Klokke &Pauline C. M. Lunsingh Scheurleer, {{P.|154-157|qq=}}</ref> .
At the [[Kasuga-taisha]] shrine in {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|qid=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}, he is said to be the same as the [[Shinto]] god of martial arts {{ill|10=fr|11=Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi|en|User:Immanuelle/draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=fr|qq=}}, probably because he wears a deer skin which was the god's mount in Nara. <ref> {{ill|Allan Grapard|en|Allan Grapard|qid=Q4730640|lt=Allan G. Grapard|1=Allan Grapard}} ''The Protocol of the Gods: A Study of the Kasuga Cult in Japanese History'', University of California press {{P.|82-83|qq=}}   </ref>
== Equivalents ==
{{ill|Kasuga-no-Kami|simple|User:Immanuelle/Kasuga-no-Kami|en:draft|Kasuga-no-Kami|ja|春日神|1=Kasuga-no-Kami|2=ja|3=春日神|4=fr|5=Kasuga no Kami|6=zh|7=春日權現|8=wuu|9=春日權現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga-no-Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga-no-Kami|qq=}} has five components each of which has a corresponding Buddha.<ref name="Ten Grotenhuis-1999a">{{Cite book|title=Japanese mandalas : representations of sacred geography|last=Ten Grotenhuis|first=Elizabeth|date=1999|publisher=University of Hawai'i Press|isbn=0824820002|oclc=39181008|qq=}}</ref>
{| class="wikitable"
!Shrine facility
![[Shinto]] part
!{{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} part
|-
|first hall<ref name="www.kasugataisha.or.jp">{{Cite web|title=Main Sanctuary|url=http://www.kasugataisha.or.jp/guidance/map3/k_map01.html|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190624041142/http://www.kasugataisha.or.jp/guidance/map3/k_map01.html|archive-date=2019-06-24|access-date=2019-03-28|website=www.kasugataisha.or.jp|qq=}}</ref>
|{{ill|Takemikazuchi|qid=Q1502690|lt=Takemikazuchi-o|en|User:Immanuelle/draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takemikazuchi|qq=|1=Takemikazuchi}}
|{{ill|Amoghapasha|ja|不空羂索観音|fr|Amoghapasha Lokeshvara|id|Arca Amoghapasa|jv|Arca Amoghapasa|zh|不空羂索觀音|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amoghapasha|qq=}}<ref>https://kuscholarworks.ku.edu/bitstream/1808/17426/1/Fowler.pdf</ref>
|-
|second hall<ref name="www.kasugataisha.or.jp" />
|{{ill|Futsunushi|en|User:Immanuelle/draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsunushi|qq=}}
|{{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|User:Immanuelle/draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bhaisajyaguru|qq=}}
|-
|third hall<ref name="www.kasugataisha.or.jp" />
|{{ill|Ame-no-Koyane|en|User:Immanuelle/draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Koyane|qq=}}
|{{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|User:Immanuelle/draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kṣitigarbha|qq=}}
|-
|fourth hall<ref name="www.kasugataisha.or.jp" />
|{{ill|Himegami|en|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Himegami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himegami|qq=}}
|{{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|User:Immanuelle/draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ekādaśamukha|qq=}}
|-
|Wakamiya Shrine<ref name="https">https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001559851.pdf</ref><ref name="Japan Tourism Agency,Japan Tourism Agency">{{Cite web|title=Wakamiya Shrine {{!|qq=}} Search Details|url=https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/en/H30-00882.html|access-date=2024-03-20|website=Japan Tourism Agency,Japan Tourism Agency|language=en|qq=}}</ref>
|{{ill|Ame-no-Oshikumone|ja|天押雲根命|en|draft:Ame-no-Oshikumone|simple|User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Oshikumone|fr|Utilisateur:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Oshikumone|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame-no-Oshikumone|qq=}}
|{{ill|Manjushri|en|Manjushri|qid=Q471696|lt=Manjushri|1=Manjushri}} or {{ill|Avalokiteśvara|en|User:Immanuelle/draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Avalokiteśvara|qq=}}.<ref name="Giolai-2020">{{Cite journal|last=Giolai|first=Andrea|date=2020-09-22|title=Encounters with the Past: Fractals and Atmospheres at Kasuga Wakamiya Onmatsuri|url=https://brill.com/view/journals/jrj/9/1-3/article-p213_8.xml|journal=Journal of Religion in Japan|volume=9|issue=1-3|pages=213–247|doi=10.1163/22118349-00901002|issn=2211-8349|qq=}}</ref>
|}
